title: Expressing support for the designation of May as "National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month".
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make May a special month to teach people about bladder cancer. Bladder cancer makes hundreds of thousands of Americans sick and kills more than seventeen thousand people every year. The bill wants people and groups to learn more about this disease and do activities in May to help fight bladder cancer. It hopes that by having a special awareness month, more people will understand how serious bladder cancer is and work together to help patients.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for making May into National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month.

sections.title: Facts About Bladder Cancer
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes these important facts about bladder cancer. Making more people aware of bladder cancer and treating it early will save thousands of lives. More than eight hundred thousand families in the United States are dealing with bladder cancer. Doctors expect that more than eighty four thousand eight hundred seventy people in the United States will be diagnosed with bladder cancer in twenty twenty five. That means over two hundred thirty Americans will get this diagnosis every day. This includes over sixty five thousand eighty men and nineteen thousand seven hundred ninety women. Bladder cancer will kill more than seventeen thousand four hundred twenty Americans this year alone. Bladder cancer can affect anyone regardless of their age, whether they are male or female, or their race. Bladder cancer is one of the top seven most diagnosed cancers in the United States. It is one of the top four most common cancers for military veterans. When doctors find bladder cancer early, it can be treated very well. However, when it is found later, people have much lower chances of surviving. The main causes of bladder cancer are smoking and being exposed to cancer causing chemicals at work or in the environment. Studies show that firefighters, veterans, and older people get bladder cancer more often than other people. Bladder cancer symptoms like blood in urine are easy to notice. However, many Americans do not know about these warning signs and often wait too long to get tested and treated. Bladder cancer costs more money to treat per patient than most other cancers. Between fifty and eighty percent of bladder cancer cases come back after treatment, so patients need to be watched for their whole lives. While bladder cancer happens more in men, women are usually diagnosed when the cancer is more advanced. Women also tend to have worse outcomes even when their cancer is found at the same stage as men. There have been very few major improvements in bladder cancer treatment in the past thirty years. Whether a bladder cancer patient has a good quality of life and survives depends largely on new medical advances and more research funding from both the federal government and private companies. Making more people aware of bladder cancer will help doctors find it earlier and give patients better chances of surviving. In twenty twenty five, Congress will require the Department of Veterans Affairs to greatly increase bladder cancer treatments for veterans who were exposed to toxins from burn pits under a law called the Honoring our PACT Act of twenty twenty two. This fulfills a promise the nation made to veterans decades ago. The nation has experience diagnosing and caring for Vietnam veterans who were exposed to cancer causing chemicals like Agent Orange and Agent Blue. The Congressionally Directed Medical Research Programs work with the National Institutes of Health to fund high risk, high reward research that could one day find a cure for bladder cancer. Throughout May every year, survivors, caregivers, and loved ones come together for walks in communities across the United States. These walks raise awareness for bladder cancer and raise money for bladder cancer research. The Bladder Cancer Advocacy Network has worked for twenty years to make more people aware of bladder cancer. They also give patients the knowledge, resources, and support they need to deal with their bladder cancer journey. The Bladder Cancer Advocacy Network funds cutting edge research every year to search for better treatments and a final cure for bladder cancer. They work every day to raise public awareness, support patients, and increase research. May would be a good month to designate as National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Resolves to Do
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making May into National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month. The House supports the goals and ideas of National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month. The House calls on the people of the United States, interested groups, the research community, and affected people to promote awareness of bladder cancer and help others understand how the disease affects patients, their families, caregivers, and their communities. The House asks these groups to take an active role in the fight to end bladder cancer. The House asks them to observe National Bladder Cancer Awareness Month with appropriate ceremonies and activities.
